plan_a_advantages: Maintains run frequency critical for trail-running neuromuscular development, preserving running-specific adaptations during recovery rather than deferring them; Plan B's shorter early runs on fewer days risks losing skill and stride efficiency.
Longer trail sessions (75–110+ min) and trail work on back-to-back weekend days (3/28–29, 4/4–5) build more elevation-specific endurance and climbing economy relevant to the 1500m goal.
Clearer progressive structure with consistent weekday easy runs (40 min at RPE 2–3/10) that remain safe for recovery while supporting aerobic base; easier to execute reliably throughout the block.
4/8 tempo session includes modification option if fatigue remains, so Plan A is still adaptable without requiring the overly conservative posture Plan B adopts from the start.
plan_b_advantages: More conservative early-week run durations (20 min vs 40 min) provide an additional safety margin if the athlete's fatigued state is slower to resolve than expected.
Explicit Zone 2 HR guidance and detailed post-session recovery/fueling notes (e.g., 'carbs and protein within 1–2 hours') offer clearer execution cues for a recovering athlete.
Shorter trail run durations may reduce recovery burden if fatigue indicators (RHR spike, poor sleep) emerge during the block, allowing rapid pivot to lower demands.
reasoning: Both plans appropriately address the athlete's fatigued state with conservative intensity and only one hard session (4/8 tempo). However, Plan A better balances recovery with maintaining running frequency essential for 21k trail racing development. Plan A's 40-minute easy runs at RPE 2–3/10 remain safely conservative while preserving the neuromuscular demands of trail running. Over a 6-month preparation window, Plan A's slightly longer trail sessions and more consistent run frequency better build the elevation-specific endurance needed for 1500m gain, whereas Plan B's approach of 20-minute early runs and shorter trail sessions (50–60 min vs 75–85 min) may unnecessarily limit progression for an athlete with strong historical trail capacity.
